reasoning: The US national average interest rate for savings accounts (SNDR) was 0.400% as of our most recent knowledge cutoff. The Federal Reserve has been actively cutting its benchmark federal funds rate (with cuts in September and late October 2025, and further cuts projected in 2025/2026). Savings deposit yields are strongly positively correlated with the federal funds rate and are already facing downward pressure as banks adjust their cost of funds. Consequently, a meaningful increase in the national average savings rate is highly unlikely in this monetary environment. However, there is a small amount of short-term noise and lag in bank responses (which caused a tiny uptick from 0.38% to 0.40% earlier in 2025). Taking this low volatility into account, the probability of the rate moving strictly higher than 0.40% by December 2025 is quite small. Following standard forecasting methodology and the instruction to increase uncertainty for longer horizons, the probability increases slightly towards 0.5 for the February 2026 forecast date.
